explanation: AutoFilter operates on a data list, so it sits on the Data menu (Data > Filter > AutoFilter in menu-era Excel; Data tab > Filter in the ribbon). Tools held options and macros, Insert held objects and functions.
Ctrl+P is the standard Print shortcut across Office and almost every Windows application. The distractors are other shortcuts: Ctrl+V pastes and Alt+F4 closes the application.

Wrap Text (Format Cells > Alignment) breaks long text onto multiple lines inside the cell boundary and grows the row height to fit. It is a cell-level alignment setting and has nothing to do with page margins or the file.
Yes. Insert > Object or Paste Special embeds or links a live Excel worksheet inside a Word document via OLE, so the sheet stays editable in place.
Bullets and Numbering is paragraph formatting, so in classic Word menus it lives under Format (the option labelled C here). Modern ribbon Word moved it to Home > Paragraph.
Ctrl+B makes the selected text and numbers bold. Ctrl+U is the underline shortcut, which is the distractor in option C.
Yes. Word table columns can be removed with Table > Delete > Columns (or right-click the column and choose Delete Columns).

Print Preview renders the whole file as it will appear on the printed page (pagination, margins, headers and footers) without sending anything to the printer. Option B describes the Print command itself, and option C wrongly narrows the preview to tables only.
The Office Assistant (Clippy) surfaced context-sensitive help topics and usage tips. It did not automate commands (that is what macros do) or save files automatically (that is AutoSave/AutoRecover).
WordArt inserts decorative text as a graphic object with effects such as curving, shadowing and 3-D. Shading text with color is a separate Highlight/Shading command, which is the trap in option C.
Subscript lowers the text below the baseline, as in the 2 of H2O. Option A is the definition of superscript (raising above the baseline, as in x squared), the classic subscript/superscript swap.
Yes. PowerPoint supports adding audio to a slide via Insert > Movies and Sounds (Insert > Audio in newer versions).
Yes. Images are added with Insert > Picture, either from a file or from clip art.
Yes. Format > Background (Design > Format Background in newer versions) changes a slide's background color, gradient, texture or picture fill.
Yes. PowerPoint ships an organization chart tool, reached via Insert > Diagram in older versions and Insert > SmartArt > Hierarchy in newer ones.

The Relationships window defines joins between the objects that hold data, linking a primary key in one table to a foreign key in another (queries can also be placed there). Forms and reports only display data, so they have no relationships to define, which rules out B and C.
Yes. Forms are the Access user interface bound to a table or query, used both to view existing records and to key in new ones.
No, the direction is backwards. A form's record source is a table or a query; reports are output-only consumers of data and can never act as a data source for a form.
Queries retrieve, filter and join data held in tables (or in other queries). Forms and reports consume query output rather than store data, so they are not things you query.

Yes. Design View is the authoring surface where a report's layout, sections and bound controls are built and edited. A wizard or AutoReport can generate a first draft, but it hands the result to Design View for actual design work.
